title: izba dogfood journeys (Phase 1 output / Phase 2 input)
description: A feature's user journeys: goals a real user would have given the spec's promises, decomposed into ordered intent+expectation steps. Produced by the local Phase 1 (intent extraction) harness; consumed by the Phase 2 (journey execution) runner.

feature.description: Human-readable name of the feature under test.

journey_id.description: Stable, unique-within-feature kebab-case id. Used for sharding and loop-dedup.

rationale.description: Why this journey exists: the spec promise / expectation it probes.

modality.description: Action surface this journey runs against. 'cli' (default) = izba shell commands via run_journeys.py; 'gui' = the Tauri app driven through a browser via run_gui_journeys.py. Absent ⇒ treated as 'cli'.

tier.description: Progressive-exploration depth (Phase 2 sequencing). 'smoke' = shallow happy-path + capability probes run first and cheap; 'core' = main feature coverage; 'deep' = adversarial / edge / multi-step. Deeper tiers are dispatched only after the prior tier's gating journeys established the prerequisite capabilities (post in-place fix). Optional; absent ⇒ treated as 'core'.

establishes.description: Capability ids this journey proves usable when it genuinely succeeds (e.g. 'install-tooling-under-enforce', 'tls-verifies-under-enforce'). Smoke journeys establish the prerequisites deeper journeys depend on. Optional; kebab-case capability tokens shared across journeys of one feature.

requires.description: Capability ids that must be established (by an earlier-tier journey, possibly after an in-place fix) before this journey is worth running. If a required capability is a confirmed unfixable blocker, the sequencer/orchestrator defers this journey and records why (never silently). Optional.

gating.description: When true, this journey is a gate for its tier: advancing to the next tier requires it to genuinely succeed (or its blocker to be fixed in-place). Use sparingly — only for the capability prerequisites deeper tiers truly depend on. Optional; default false.

seed_files.description: Precondition files materialized into the journey's workdir (HOST-side — the dir the Actor's shell runs in on the machine running izba, never inside a guest) BEFORE its first step: a map of relative path -> file content. Models an environment precondition a deep journey legitimately assumes (the same contract 'establishes'/'requires' encode), NOT a hint to the Actor: seed content lives here in journeys.json, which the Actor never sees. Traversal-guarded (relative paths only, no '..' escape). Compiler rule: seed a precondition, NEVER the thing under test (e.g. seeding a valid izba.yml for a review-gate journey is a precondition; seeding it for a manifest-authoring journey would destroy the test). Optional.

kind.description: Where the expectation was traced from (spec | pr | greptile review | --help topic | README).

ref.description: Pointer into the source (doc + section, PR number, review id, help topic).

intent.description: What the user wants to do at this step (intent, NOT a literal command). The Actor chooses the concrete izba invocation.

expect.description: The expected observable outcome of this step, in user-observable terms (judged by the Phase-3 trajectory-skeptic against product state — NOT a literal exit-code assertion).

core.description: Marks this step as DECISIVE: the functional assertion graded here governs the journey's positive/negative tally (setup/recovery noise in non-core steps is downgraded to soft). If no step in a journey is marked core, the LAST step is treated as decisive. Optional; default false.

expect_exit.description: Declarative exit-code assertion that supersedes the fragile 'expect' keyword heuristic when present: an integer N grades the step's final action as PASS iff it exited exactly N; the string 'nonzero' grades PASS iff it exited non-zero (the way to assert an expected refusal, e.g. `izba ssh NAME -- false`). When absent, the 'expect' English-keyword path is the fallback (backward compatible). When the step also declares expect_cmd_re, the graded action is the last one matching that regex instead of the final action. Optional.

expect_cmd_re.description: Optional regex anchoring WHICH of the step's actions the functional/expect_exit verdict is graded against: the LAST action whose command matches. Without it the step's final action is graded — which false-fires when the Actor runs a trailing verify (e.g. `izba ls`) after the intent-bearing command. Compiler rule: set it on refusal/expect_exit steps to the distinctive token of the command under test (e.g. 'promote'); never anchor to an exact full command line (that would leak a prescription to the swarm's benefit — the regex lives here, invisible to the Actor). Decisive (core) steps SHOULD declare expect_cmd_re: it also lets the runner credit a decisive assertion satisfied under an earlier step instead of flagging unreached_decisive. Anchor the pattern to the full product invocation (e.g. 'izba promote' or a subcommand-specific regex), never a bare token like 'izba' — over-broad patterns can credit the decisive step from an unrelated command; every credit is recorded in the bundle's decisive_credits for the skeptic to audit.

expect_text.description: GUI decisive grading hook — the GUI analog of expect_exit/expect_cmd_re. An EXACT, case-insensitive LITERAL substring that must appear in the captured page text (document.body.innerText, the per-action `page_text` — deliberately NOT the accessibility marks: Radix/shadcn dialogs and plain-<div> outcome copy are invisible to the a11y snapshot, see gui_oracles). Graded on a `core: true` step of a modality:'gui' journey when the journey did NOT drive manifest_diff (manifest_truth then takes precedence); the CLI runner ignores it. Search window: every page-text capture at/after this core step — the step's own opening snapshot (so pure-observation steps grade), each of its and any later action's captures, and the final post-settle capture (an async create/boot may render the outcome after the last action). Compiler rules: (1) choose an OUTCOME string the app renders only when the asserted outcome actually happened — banner/result copy ('Promoted 2 change(s).'), a rendered row's name+status — NEVER dialog chrome (headings, button/field/tab labels render whether or not the outcome happened: the known dom_expect chrome false-pass class from PR #121/#130); (2) this is an exact-substring match, not fuzzy keywords — no keyword soup. The literal token {workspace} is substituted with the journey's absolute workspace path. A core step carrying neither expect_text nor expect_state in a journey that drives no manifest_diff flips unreached_decisive. Invisible to the Actor (fair-test boundary, like expect_cmd_re). Optional.

sandbox.description: The sandbox name the assertion targets — normally the literal name the step's intent prescribes (e.g. 'manifest-sync-demo'). The literal token {workspace} is substituted with the journey's absolute workspace path (same single-token templating as intent/expect).

exists.description: true ⇒ the named sandbox must be present in the daemon's end-of-journey sandbox list; false ⇒ it must be absent (the way to assert a completed remove).

status.description: The sandbox's reconciled status must equal this string exactly (e.g. 'running', 'stopped' — the product's own status vocabulary, status_disk falling back to status_daemon). Implies existence: an absent sandbox fails a status assertion.

name.description: The persistent (named) volume the assertion targets — the literal volume name the step's intent prescribes (e.g. 'detach-vol'), never a guest mountpoint path. No {workspace} substitution (volume names are compiler-chosen literals).

exists.description: true ⇒ the named volume must be present in the daemon's end-of-journey `izba volume ls` truth (e.g. a persistent volume surviving sandbox rm); false ⇒ it must be absent (the way to assert a completed volume rm/prune).

attached_to.description: A sandbox name ⇒ the volume must exist AND be referenced by that sandbox's config (`izba volume ls`'s USED BY column — a Saved attach that actually landed in daemon truth); null ⇒ the volume must exist but be referenced by NO sandbox (detached-but-existing — the way to assert a Saved detach landed, instead of crediting the Volumes tab's staged, unsaved 'No volumes' text). Implies existence: an absent volume fails an attached_to assertion, including attached_to: null.

volume.description: Optional persistent-volume assertion, graded from the SAME end-of-journey state evidence (the `izba volume ls` capture — product truth, never UI text). At least one of exists/attached_to must be declared. Composes with the sibling sandbox exists/status keys: ALL declared assertions must pass. Compiler rule: use this (not expect_text over the Volumes tab) to grade attach/detach outcomes — the tab renders staged, unsaved rows that expect_text would falsely credit (the volumes-detach cheat class).

expect_state.description: GUI decisive grading hook — a small daemon-truth assertion graded against the runner's end-of-journey state evidence (the post-settle `izba __reconcile` snapshot + `izba volume ls` capture: product ground truth, not what the UI happens to render). Graded on a `core: true` step of a modality:'gui' journey when the journey did NOT drive manifest_diff (manifest_truth then takes precedence); the CLI runner ignores it. ALL declared sub-assertions (and any sibling expect_text) must pass for the decisive step to grade positive; a failed assertion flips the journey via a decisive functional candidate; an ungradable one (the reconcile snapshot itself errored, or a volume assertion with no usable `izba volume ls` evidence) degrades the journey via a flipping infra candidate — never a silent pass. A failing assertion is first re-sampled across a bounded settle window (--expect-state-settle-s, default 45s): lifecycle teardown and volume-save operations land asynchronously, so only divergence that PERSISTS across the settle flips — both the first and settled samples are recorded in the bundle for the skeptic. Invisible to the Actor (fair-test boundary). Optional.

seed_files.description: Precondition files materialized into the journey's workdir (HOST-side, same target as the journey-level field of the same name) IMMEDIATELY BEFORE THIS STEP's first action — not before step 0: a map of relative path -> file content. Models drift introduced MID-journey (e.g. the agent edits izba.yml between steps to exercise the diff/promote reconciler on a change it didn't itself make), the same way the journey-level field models a precondition present from the start. NOT a hint to the Actor: seed content lives here in journeys.json, which the Actor never sees, and the write itself is not a graded action — the step's functional assertion is still graded on the Actor's own command, never on this write. Traversal-guarded (relative paths only, no '..' escape). Compiler rule: seed a precondition/drift event, NEVER the thing under test. Optional.
